metadata.from: Bob (Robert E. Hanifen) — confirmed by return address on envelope: 'Robert E. Hanifen 12170600, 3020 Ord & Mt. AF, APO 528, Postmaster New York, N.Y.' and signed 'Bobby'
metadata.to: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.) — letter opens 'Dear Dad' and is addressed to Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
metadata.location_written_from: Likely Italy, near Caserta/Naples area — mentions Red Cross in Caserta, Naples off limits, and APO 528 (Italy theater)
metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.postmark: Partially legible circular postmark reading 'POSTAL PAY... A.P.O. 51 [?]' — APO postmark, date partially illegible
metadata.envelope_notes: Free frank / military postage mark visible. Return address: Robert E. Hanifen 12170600, 3020 Ord & Mt. AF, APO 528, Postmaster New York, N.Y. Addressed to Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa.

meeting a girl named Anna at the Red Cross in Caserta

metadata.mood_tone: Casual, upbeat, and conversational with touches of humor; slightly homesick but generally lighthearted; candid about military life and social observations
metadata.legibility_notes: Generally readable cursive but some words require inference; page 4 has significant bleed-through from reverse side making lower portion difficult; some words on page 3 partially obscured; 'Macfarlane' spelling uncertain; '3/4 ton station wagon' notation uses superscript fractions
metadata.historical_context: Written in March 1946, shortly after WWII ended; Bob is still stationed in Italy (APO 528, Naples/Caserta area) doing motor pool inspections; Naples placed off limits due to high VD rates — a common postwar military public health measure in occupied Italy; reference to 3020 Ordnance and Maintenance Air Force unit.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS:
Robert E. Hanifen 12170600
3020 Ord & Mt. AF
APO 528
Postmaster New York, N.Y.

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

POSTMARK: [Circular military postmark, partially legible: 'POSTAL PAY... A.P.O. 51[?]']
STAMP: Free military frank / postal meter marks visible

4 March, 1946

Dear Dad

     Just got back from the Show. State Fair it sorta made me a little homesick to see the old fairgrounds again. We inspected the Battalion Motor Pool today it took about 1 hr. I drove about 6 different vehicles. a jeep, a 3/4 ton station wagon, a 2½ ton 6x6, a 4½ ton auto car, and a 10 ton prime mover, boy was it ever huge. Almost need a step ladder to get in it. I wrote up the report in about a hour, getting so I can do it pretty

fast now. We 20 more things to inspection this month. The Lt. is going on his vacation the last week in March. So we'll have to do it all 3 weeks.
We have two inspection in Bari one in Macfarlane [Macfarlane?]. The Lt. says we will probably fly down. There it is about 200 miles away. And the roads are pretty rough to drive. Should be a good trip. Sure hope I can get a rating of this job.
I met a pretty nice girl at the Red Cross in Caserta. Her mother was with her a pretty good sign in this country. So they tell.

II

Her name is Anna, she is really nice compared to this other woman mainly because she is clean, well clean anyway. All the boys think she is alright. I am not going to get married so don't worry. Just somebody to dance, remember the song.
Naples is off limits the 18 of March, to much V.D. says the Army. This country is all closed up. Don't work at all in the afternoon, just sit around and talk all day. Not a bad life at all. Sorta of wish there was more work

to do. All the boys are sitting here bulling about the girls back home. That all we do at night it seems, besides go to show. Well Dad goodbye for now. Lots of love

                              for now

                                        Bobby

[remainder of page is bleed-through from reverse side, illegible]

full_transcription:
4 March, 1946

Dear Dad

     Just got back from the Show. State Fair it sorta made me a little homesick to see the old fairgrounds again. We inspected the Battalion Motor Pool today it took about 1 hr. I drove about 6 different vehicles. a jeep, a 3/4 ton station wagon, a 2½ ton 6x6, a 4½ ton auto car, and a 10 ton prime mover, boy was it ever huge. Almost need a step ladder to get in it. I wrote up the report in about a hour, getting so I can do it pretty fast now. We 20 more things to inspection this month. The Lt. is going on his vacation the last week in March. So we'll have to do it all 3 weeks.
We have two inspection in Bari one in Macfarlane [Macfarlane?]. The Lt. says we will probably fly down. There it is about 200 miles away. And the roads are pretty rough to drive. Should be a good trip. Sure hope I can get a rating of this job.
I met a pretty nice girl at the Red Cross in Caserta. Her mother was with her a pretty good sign in this country. So they tell.

Her name is Anna, she is really nice compared to this other woman mainly because she is clean, well clean anyway. All the boys think she is alright. I am not going to get married so don't worry. Just somebody to dance, remember the song.
Naples is off limits the 18 of March, to much V.D. says the Army. This country is all closed up. Don't work at all in the afternoon, just sit around and talk all day. Not a bad life at all. Sorta of wish there was more work to do. All the boys are sitting here bulling about the girls back home. That all we do at night it seems, besides go to show. Well Dad goodbye for now. Lots of love

                              for now

                                        Bobby
summary: Written on March 4, 1946 from Italy, Bob (Bobby) Hanifen writes to his father about his military duties inspecting the Battalion Motor Pool, driving six different vehicles including a massive 10-ton prime mover. He mentions upcoming inspections in Bari and possibly Macfarlane (about 200 miles away) that may require flying. He casually mentions meeting a girl named Anna at the Red Cross in Caserta, reassuring his father he has no marriage plans, and notes that Naples has been placed off-limits due to high VD rates. The letter paints a picture of postwar occupation duty in Italy — relatively light work, lots of idle time, and soldiers longing for home.
